sentences.text: You know, it's strange.
When I think about that-- that day, I'm trying still to understand it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Life is precious.
And no matter what the circumstance, there's a will to live.
At least was, when you're young.
For a parent, maybe it wasn't so.
But I'm just trying to tell you how I felt.
I wanted to live.
But when I was on the truck, and I knew the ultimate end-- what it will be-- because I had watched my brother on that same truck, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a sense of relief.
There was a sense of, this is it.
Who wants to live in this kind of world, anyway?
And what am I living for?
I don't know what happened to my mother.
I don't know what happened to my father.
My brother next to me is already dead.
And so there's-- there was a resignation.
I don't know how to describe it.
But it-- it sort of felt good, really, until the SS man that knew me spotted me, and he motions for the truck to stop.
And then he came by, where they lowered the-- the closure of the truck.
And he motioned for this little kid to come down.
And when they dragged me out of the truck towards the barracks, I remember fainting.
I guess I realized that I'm going-- going to live.
And I guess it-- the emotions took over.
I don't know why I fainted.
Was I sorry that I was going to live?
I-- I can't explain it.
And, you know, slowly got back to health and did my work.
I'm going to jump a little bit ahead.
Life went on.
And with, every day, trials and tribulations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the Russians were advancing on the Eastern Front, there was a lot of commotions, and people were beginning to celebrate.
They thought, oh, great, we're going to-- to make it.
We're going to-- to survive.
One other incident that comes to mind that I should tell you that happened-- I was friends with one young fellow that was a bit older than me.
His name was Schmiel.
In-- in the munitions factory.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, these huge belts that drive the-- the machines-- when they wore out, they would be discarded-- thrown in the garbage.
So he picked up a piece of that and cut it in-- in pieces so that we could put it in the inside of our shoes.
Some of our shoes were worn down, you know, and when it was cold and wet, this sort of gave a little protection.
We would bundle up our feet in-- in paper, as well.
So he gave me some of those-- a piece-- and I put it in my pocket.
But he was spotted, taking that.
And-- and I thought that one of the SS men noticed both of us having it, but I wasn't noticed.
That day, he got picked out.
And we were made to stand in a circle, after work, after we got back to the barracks.
And this SS man said, no one steals.
He wasn't stealing.
It-- He picked it up from the garbage.
No one steals from the Third Reich.
And without any ceremony at all, he took out the revolver and shot him, point blank.
And I was quite close to him.
You know, some of the-- his blood spilled on me.
And here, again-- you know, I'm just telling you about close calls.
Here, again, I thought, uh-oh-- that's it.
That's it for me, because I thought I was next.
He didn't see me putting it in my pocket.
And so I wasn't called out next.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was devastated.
But I had already witnessed my-- the loss of my brother.
And it's amazing how you begin to harden.
You see?
And, sure, I was terribly upset.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was just another thing that happened, another thing to deal with it that you sort of put aside and immediately go on.
You develop that kind of resistance to the outside forces that prey on you.
And that is part of the dehumanization process.
You, after a while, become sort of like a zombie.
You do with your instinct.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Another incident before the advent of the Russians coming-- when there was enough ammunitions, usually towards the end of the week, I would be sent to the office for to make the bill of lading.
There was a woman there.
I don't know if she was a Pole or a German-- blonde-- the secretary.
When she-- she had very kind eyes, and she was instrumental in bringing my health back, because what she did, when she hand me the bill of lading, underneath it would be her lunch.
And it consisted of two pieces of bread, with butter and marmalade, which was unheard of.
I hadn't seen this since the start of the war, since the-- after I left home.
It had a tremendous amount of nutritional value.
But what I'm trying to say is that, in the midst of this madness, there was a spark of humanity and kindness in the most unlikely places.
Was she a Nazi?
What was this woman?
If I knew her name, and I knew where she was, I would certainly [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put forward her name to Yad Vashem, to declare her a righteous Gentile.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Although both our lives were jeopardized, I didn't think of mine.
I couldn't give a damn about it.
I wanted her lunch, no matter what cost.
But she risked her life to do this human kindness.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wish I knew where she is or who she is-- if she's still alive.
Oh, good heavens, no, absolutely not.
First of all, I would risk the-- the source, jeopardize her-- her life and mine.
I didn't tell it to anyone.
And I also ate it secretly.
And how did she know, I mean, that I would do this?
So there was-- you see that-- that jump and her faith in me, that I would be discreet enough to swallow this food and not jeopardize her.
But she took that chance.
She took that leap of faith in me.
And I never-- I can see her look, her piercing look, on me, full of compassion, full of menschlichkeit, full of-- whatever she was, she was obviously a wonderful human being.
No.
No.
People were looking at me, and-- with-- like this lady.
And at work, and so on, people were kind to me, of sorts, including some of the SS men.
You know, it was-- after they distributed the soup, for instance, at lunchtime, when we had our break-- we had about 20 minutes to eat it-- if there was anything left over, I would get it.
There was very seldom left over, but sometimes there was.
There was a little-- little something at the end of the pot.
And they would-- so-- a little bit on a big pot that's left over, you know, you start taking it out, it's quite a bit.
And it was very, very, very sustaining and probably instrumental in-- in keeping me alive.
There were all kinds of conversations.
There were conversations in the camp when we went to sleep, after they-- there was a bulb [INAUDIBLE].
When they say 'lights out,' you know, you think somebody went up there and unscrewed the bulb.
Sometimes we didn't even want to do this, because then you have all these bugs descending on it.
So sometimes we even left the lights on.
But there were these groups of people, you know.
There were-- they come from all walks of life.
There were artists.
There were teachers.
There were, you know, writers and poets, doctors, lawyers.
And they would philosophy.
I particularly remember a certain conversation. .
I remember hearing, anybody that will be lucky enough to survive this [BACKROUND NOISE] nightmare, this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] will live in paradise.
There will be just absolutely beautiful life, a beauti-- hunger will completely disappear.
There will not be any hunger.
People will be kind to one another.
There will be no discrimination as we know it.
It'll be pure paradise.
And I remember thinking, I want to survive this.
I want to live in this kind of paradise.
Particularly what attracted me is that nobody's going to be hungry.
There's going to be plenty of food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Unfortunately, you know, as we know, you turn on the television and there is still-- you see Biafra.
You still see ethnic cleansing.
And, as a survivor, you know, I-- I identify with this kid behind barbed wires.
And it made me furious, you know.
I wrote to-- to the member of parliament, because-- I said, do something!
Don't let this kind of thing reoccur.
You know, that's another thing that's totally amazing.
There must have been some sort of a-- an underground grapevine that people-- we knew the progress on the Eastern Front.
The Russians were advancing.
And the-- we knew about Normandy landing, you know.
News used to come in.
I think that somebody must have had a wireless, because these things were-- were coming to us.

The other thing that I remember very clearly-- and that what sort of prompted me to go to schools and talk about it-- is that I remember one of them say, anyone that survives this will have a duty and an obligation to speak about it and tell the free world what happened.
And I remember one of the guys pointing to me.
He says, we have a little kid.
He might survive.
And the other guy said, don't bet on it.
Forget it.
And that was sort of a down, for me.
He says, well-- and I thought to this-- hey, he thinks that I'm going to survive.
I-- sometimes I never doubted it.
I didn't even think about it.
You just lived day to day and hoped for the best.
And so that sort of came back.
We had a teacher in Eckville, Alberta, by the name of Keegstra.
You might have heard about him.
Now, he was teaching his students that the Holocaust is just a hoax and it didn't happen.
I got very angry.
I thought to myself, what happened to my brothers and to my family, to my entire family?
They're all wiped out.
And the Holocaust didn't happen?
So I thought that I've been comfortable enough.
I've got to share this with some of the students and tell them it did happen.
And if we don't watch, that it can happen again.
Let us go back to when you were in camp.
What kept you going?
Was it your belief in God?
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't think much about this.
You know, what kept me going is that I wanted to go home after it's all over.
And, in my mind, my perception was that my family's going to wait for me, because, you know, I was the kid-- the little kid-- and I wasn't going to make it.
They were all worried about me.
So I was going to march in there.
Surprise!
I'm alive!
I'm here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

Can you imagine that, when I wanted to do this, finally, and I found out there's no home to go home to and that everything that I've known, all the people that I played with, and all my family are gone, annihilated.
But that's another story.
It took a long time for me to come-- to get in touch with those kind of feeling and to-- and to come to terms with that.
That was most difficult.
And I'll tell you later on who was responsible to make us human again, because we weren't.
We were dehumanized to such an extent that we began to believe it.
You were just a zombie, automatically doing the varied things to survive.
And so, when the Russians started advancing, we thought, hang on!
You're going to make it.
We were marched out of Skarzysko, loaded on trains, packed like cattle.
Off we went, deeper into Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were dying left and right.
We didn't have any food, nor drink.
People were frightened.
They died of what have you.
But we finally got loaded--
The first time wasn't too bad.
I think we were there about a day and a half.
So that wasn't too tragic.
There were some dead, but not-- most of us survived that trip.
And then we ended up in a place called Przedborz.
In Przedborz, we were in a field in the middle of nowhere.
And we were digging antitank ditches-- these huge ditches.
People were really despondent, some of them.
And if you wanted to end it all, what you did is walk away.
And the guards-- sometimes they asked you to stop; other times, they would say nothing and just shoot you.
It was a miserable time.
The lice were just-- I remember going to the washroom and just brushing my-- the lice off me.
It wasn't-- you know, at first, when you-- lice, you sort of go into the curves-- the sides-- the stitches, where the stitches-- the seams that were there.
But, after a while, they're just all over you.
And they just suck at you, all the time, you know, just blood all over from-- it's just a horrible thing.
We worked in pairs.
The man that I worked with threw his shovel down in the dirt.
And he looked at me and says, kid, it's all yours.
And he had a smile on his face, and he walked away.
The guard was being human.
He said, stop!
He just kept walking.
And, of course, he was shot.
He wanted it that way.
You know, I did, but it escapes me.
I worked with him that-- that particular day.
Once they shoot somebody, they get sort of in a trigger-happy mood.
And everybody digs that much faster-- including myself.
While I was doing this, the butt of his rifle rested on my shoulder.
And I looked back, thinking, here we go again.
I'm probably next.
But why-- I'm thinking-- very quickly, I said, why is he hesitating?
He motions me to follow him.
I follow him.
He goes at where he had a knapsack, at the edge of the-- of an incline, there.
He bends down, takes out an apple, and gives it to me and motions me to go back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
You know, I wonder about this.
And I'm trying to think, why did he do that?
Was there sort of, again, a little bit of humanity, a moment of reflection, looking at me, thinking-- maybe he has a son that I reminded him of.
What went-- I know what went through my mind.
But I was trying to picture my-- what went through his mind, for him doing this?
Giving-- first of all, I enjoyed the apple, but that's beside the point.
He gave me a chance.
Did he say, don't do something stupid like what your friend did?
Everything isn't lost?
Courage, go on, live?
Is that what he tried to tell me?
I-- I think about it sometimes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It makes you think.
In my own mind-- you asked me about God-- I take all this into account.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I was brought up believing in God.
I'm trying to come to terms with that.
I read books.
I'm trying to find out, why did it happen?
I certainly think that God didn't want it that way.
I came across something the other day that helps me to maybe rationalize and-- and to come to terms with it.
There was this Jew in the Warsaw ghetto who wrote, accusing God.
He said, God, how can you [BACKROUND NOISE] stand, looking children being thrown from third-story windows and-- and soldiers with bayonets catching them?
How can you?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You are a compassionate God.
And so he was very angry with God.
And then, in his group, when there was the uprising of the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all his friends had died, and he had only a short while to live.
And he wrote another note to God.
And he said, God, forgive me for being angry with you.
I-- You see what they do to our people, and I have witnessed all this what's happening all around us.
And I've come to the conclusion that you are not their god.
Because, if you were, you wouldn't allow it to happen.
Therefore, you must be my god.
Yes, particularly after the War, too.
During, I didn't have time to reflect on it.
But afterwards, when I was left all by myself, with only my sister, I was angry.
When I realized the enormity of the deed, I was angry.
Przedborz?
Not very long.
We were there for the summer months, and then we went to Czestochowa.
There was another ammunitions factory.
I worked there in the automaten.
I was, again, mechanically inclined.
There were girls that manned these machines.
And I had a screwdriver in my hand.
And any time the machine would get stuck, I would unscrew the major parts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take out the blockage, reassemble the machine, and off it went.
And so, again, people admired how fast I did this, and it gave me a-- a sort of-- a reason to-- to be left alive.
Yes.
There was sort of the same barracks, you know, that I've described.
And, again, the Russians were advancing.
And we got taken out of Czestochowa, packed into cattle trains.
And that was a horrible trip.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The-- the hunger and-- and the cold, and-- it was just, just awful.
Some--
Just a jacket, [BACKROUND NOISE] with a pair of pants.
They were long pants that were up to here, you know, and I had a rope tied around, because they weren't my own clothes anymore.
I had lost them in-- in one of the showers that I went through.
That's also another story.
I don't know if you want me to say that.
My father had put pictures and some jewelry in-- in the lining of-- of my jacket.
And those-- you know, that was my sort of security.

And when we were put through the showers, you undress, and you go through the showers-- At that time, we already knew about some of the showers being disguised as, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know-- uh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a-- not-- not a crematorium, the, uh-- the gas chamber.
They were disguised, these gas chambers, you know, because they didn't want any panic.
So, with this knowledge, suddenly we're in this huge shower--
That was when we got-- there was one in Czestochowa, but there's also one in Buchenwald.
It was extremely big.
And we were suspicious.
So I remember, all-- we were there-- the first time that I lost my clothes was in Czestochowa.
So-- piece of soap, naked, with a whole bunch of people here, and we wait.
And finally we see the water come down and not-- not the gas, and we-- ah!
We're going to live!
We're going to live.
So the panic sort of subsided.
And then we go out in the other side.
You have a pile of clothes.
And everybody scrams there to try and find something to fit.
There were no children, so, naturally, when I found a pair of pants, they came up to my-- to my neck, which was fine, in a way, because it kept me warm.
And a jacket that was six times the size.
Who knows?
Probably-- what they did-- the Germans find out-- found out, you know, that there are gold and and silver that you can find in these clothes.
So they had a group of people, inmates, that were ripping these apart, under their visual inspection.
And so that's how they-- the pictures got destroyed.
And what I am sorry about, and what I-- I'd give anything.
Let them have the gold.
That's not important.
It's the pictures-- the pictures.
What I wouldn't do to look at the face of my mum, who I adored, and the picture of my father and my brothers.
I have one picture that somebody recognized after the war, when they walked on the street of Skarzysko, and they saw a picture of my oldest brother in uniform.
And he recognized it and sent it to my sister and I.
That's my treasure.
When I look at it, I-- when I first got it, I looked at it for days!
I couldn't-- you know, every day I'd look at it for-- and I'd look at it, stare at it, look at his eyes, look at his nose.
It's so precious-- a picture.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Holocaust teaches us so many things about life and what's important and what isn't important.
We tend to forget this, sometimes when we get caught up in our daily life.
And we forget what's important.
What is important.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So, in Czestochowa, again, when the Russians were advancing, we thought, my gosh, we're going to make it!
It wasn't to be.
We got-- We're going to go to break now.
We go-- OK.
